15. Transfer Learning

  • In “Representation Learning” we mentioned how pre-training + fine tuning enables DL for downstream tasks with small data. For example, pre-training can be genre recognition and post-training acan be preference prediction.

  • More generaly transfer learning is interested in:

    • Transferring knowledge about the mappign (finetuning and domain adaptation)
    • Transfering knowledge about the outputs (distillation)
    • Transferring knowledge about the inputs (generative models)
    • Learning to learn quickly (meta-learning)

15.1. Efficient finetuning

  • Linear probing: simply use linear output layer
  • Low-rank Adaption (LoRA): instead of directly updating the weights , use where , and . Empirically, finetuning modifies weights only slightly. LoRA a pproximates fine tuning while training of parameters.

  • Avoiding overfitting

    • Freeze parameters
    • Small learning rates
    • Early stopping
    • Joint training on original and new data to reduce catastrophic forgetting

15.2. [REVIEW] Domain Adaptation

When source and target distributions differ, finetuning alone fails.

  1. Feature-space alignment: One solution mentioned in the generalization lecture is to learn mappings such that the source and target samples map to similar distributions.
  2. Domain-adversarial neural network (DANN) adds a domain classifier after the feature extractor and penalizes when the features are informative of domain:

  1. [REVIEW] Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA): What if you only have labelled data in the source domain? If the source model is somewhat accurate on the target then we try this:

    1. Start with source trained model
    2. Pseudo-label the target data. Examples with low confidence are ignored
    3. Train a student on pseudo-labelled data

    4. Make the teacher become an exponential moving average of the student. I.e.

      (47)

    5. Student simultaneously trains on target with pseudo-labels and source with true labels
    6. Distill teacher probabilities to student. Algin internal features of student and teacher.

15.3. Distillation

  • Classical distillation: Traing a smaller model to reproduce the results of a bigger model. The bigger model label includes probabilities for other classes and is thus more informative. The Hinton 2015 paper shows lowe train accuracy but higher test accuracy.

    • Used for model compression (distillation refers explicitly to training a student model from a teacher).

  • Cross-modal distillation: Use a modality with labels to supervise a modality without: e.g. pass unlabelled video through visual recognition network. Also pass it through a SoundNet architecture and take KL divergence with object and scene distribution so that sound model learns to correlate sound patterns with visual semantics.

  • Distilling an ensemble: hopefully the errors cancel out
  • Contrastive representation distillation: treat teacher vs student view as augmentation and force embeddings to match.
  • Unlike distillation, compression uses pruning (zero weights that increase loss under a second-order approximationIs this in ) or quantization to reduce computational cost

15.4. Generative Models as Data

  • Empirically: labelling a single image generated by a GAN gives as much as one hundred human labeled real images

  • DatasetGAN: GANs have internal information

    1. Generate a synthetic image using GAN
    2. Annotate the image manually
    3. Extract internal GAN feature vectors at each pixel
    4. Train a small MLP to map GAN features to pixel label.
    5. APply this MLP to more GAN images to auto label stuff
  • But we need enough data to train a good generator so this is circular.
  • [TODO] personalized generation

15.5. [REVIEW] Meta-Learning

  • MAML (Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning): Learn an initialization such that a single or few gradient steps on a new task produce a good task-spacific model.

    1. Starting with a distribution of tasks, sample a task and do one gradient step on a small training set for that task.
    2. Evaluate on the task’s test set and update the original parameters by minimizing post-adaptation loss.
  • Intuition: This rewards parameters that become good after one step and punishes parameters that don’t. This makes gradients point at their respective optima.
